title: Unauthenticated Free Shipping Shortcode AJAX Handler - Missing Nonce and No Input Sanitization
description: The AJAX handler `update_oceanwp_woo_free_shipping_left_shortcode` is registered for both authenticated (`wp_ajax_`) and unauthenticated (`wp_ajax_nopriv_`) requests. It accepts `$_POST['content']` and `$_POST['content_rech_data']` with no nonce check and no sanitization before passing them into `oceanwp_woo_free_shipping_left_shortcode()`. The raw values are subject only to downstream filtering: `$_POST['content_rech_data']` is placed directly into `$atts['content_reached']` without intermediate sanitization, and `$_POST['content']` undergoes `str_replace('+','%',...)` before being assigned to `$atts['content']`. Both are later processed through `wp_kses_post()` and `esc_attr()` within the shortcode function. Because `wp_send_json()` JSON-encodes the returned HTML string, the output lands in a JavaScript context where client-side code is expected to render it as markup. The severity depends on how the calling JavaScript handles the response. If it uses `innerHTML` or equivalent, `wp_kses_post` stripping may be the only defense, but HTML event handlers allowed by `wp_kses_post` (e.g., via allowed tags) could survive. More concretely, the lack of nonce means any page on the internet can cause a logged-in visitor's browser to trigger this endpoint (CSRF), and the unsanitized `content_reached` with no nonce protection means data flows from an external attacker into WooCommerce cart display logic.
exploitation_scenario: An attacker hosts a malicious page that submits a cross-origin POST to `wp-admin/admin-ajax.php?action=update_oceanwp_woo_free_shipping_left_shortcode` with arbitrary `content` and `content_rech_data` values. No nonce is required. The JSON response contains HTML constructed from attacker-controlled input filtered only by `wp_kses_post`. If the JS consumer uses `innerHTML` to render the response, and if `wp_kses_post` allows any event-bearing attributes via its defaults, stored or reflected XSS may result. At a minimum, the endpoint leaks WooCommerce cart threshold data to unauthenticated callers.
remediation: 1. Add `check_ajax_referer()` at the start of the handler using a nonce created via `wp_create_nonce()` and included in the front-end JS. 2. Sanitize `$_POST['content']` and `$_POST['content_rech_data']` with `sanitize_text_field()` or `wp_kses_post()` immediately on input before passing to the shortcode function. 3. Validate that WooCommerce is active (the check already exists in the called function but should be earlier). Review how the calling JavaScript renders the JSON response to ensure `textContent` or equivalent is used rather than `innerHTML`.
false_positive_reason: Not a false positive. The missing nonce is confirmed by code inspection. The downstream wp_kses_post filtering mitigates direct XSS but does not address CSRF or information leakage, and the sanitization is applied after user data is already placed into the shortcode attribute array.

title: Mautic Subscribe Nopriv Handler - Nonce Tied to Onboarding Wizard Context
description: The AJAX handler `ajax_subscribe` is registered for both authenticated and unauthenticated users via `wp_ajax_oe_mautic_subscribe` and `wp_ajax_nopriv_oe_mautic_subscribe`. It calls `check_ajax_referer('owp-onboarding', 'security')` before processing. The email is validated with `is_email()` and sanitized with `sanitize_email()`. The nonce `owp-onboarding` is generated during the onboarding wizard flow and included in the wizard's JavaScript. If this nonce is exposed in page source or JavaScript to unauthenticated visitors (e.g., if the wizard page is publicly accessible), unauthenticated users could subscribe arbitrary email addresses to the OceanWP Mautic mailing list without consent. The nonce window is 24 hours. The impact is limited to email subscription spam rather than site compromise.
exploitation_scenario: An attacker visits a page that includes the onboarding wizard JavaScript, extracts the `owp-onboarding` nonce value, then calls the AJAX endpoint with an arbitrary `email` parameter. This subscribes any target email address to OceanWP's Mautic instance at `http://mautic.oceanwp.org`. The HTTP endpoint (not HTTPS) used for the Mautic submission also means the email address is transmitted in plaintext.
remediation: 1. Remove the `wp_ajax_nopriv_` registration if this action should only be triggered during admin onboarding. 2. Change the Mautic submission URL from `http://` to `https://mautic.oceanwp.org` to prevent email addresses from being transmitted in plaintext. 3. Consider rate-limiting or bot protection on the endpoint.
false_positive_reason: Not a false positive - the nopriv registration is confirmed. However severity is low because: (a) the nonce requirement makes exploitation harder, (b) email is properly sanitized, and (c) the only impact is adding an email to a third-party mailing list.

title: MailChimp Widget Nopriv AJAX Handler - Nonce Protected, Low Risk
description: The AJAX handler `oceanwp_mailchimp_request_callback` is registered for unauthenticated requests via `wp_ajax_nopriv_oceanwp_mailchimp_request`. It validates a nonce with `check_ajax_referer('oe_mc_nonce')`. The nonce is generated server-side via `wp_create_nonce('oe_mc_nonce')` and localized into the page JavaScript only when the widget is active. The email address from `$_POST['email']` is validated with `is_email()` and sanitized with `sanitize_email()` before use. The handler is intentionally public to allow front-end newsletter subscriptions. The nonce protects against CSRF. No sensitive data is returned - only a `{status: true/false}` boolean.
exploitation_scenario: With a valid nonce obtained from the front-end page, an unauthenticated user can subscribe any valid email to the configured MailChimp list. This is the intended behavior for a newsletter signup widget. An attacker could use this to subscribe spam email addresses, but this is an expected limitation of all public newsletter forms.
remediation: No security fix required. Optionally implement rate limiting or CAPTCHA to prevent email subscription spam.
false_positive_reason: This is a false positive for security vulnerability - the nonce check and email sanitization are correctly implemented. The nopriv handler is by design for a front-end newsletter form.

title: Menu Icons Settings AJAX Handler Missing Capability Check
description: The AJAX handler `_ajax_oe_menu_icons_update_settings` (registered as `wp_ajax_oe_menu_icons_update_settings`, admin-only) verifies a nonce with `check_ajax_referer(self::UPDATE_KEY, self::UPDATE_KEY)` but does NOT check `current_user_can()` before updating the `oe-icons` option. The nonce `oe-icons-settings-update` is only generated and embedded in the nav-menus.php page, which requires the `edit_theme_options` or equivalent capability. In practice, any authenticated WordPress user who somehow obtains this nonce (e.g., via another user's browser, stored XSS payloads, or debug exposure) could modify menu icon settings. The redirect URL returned by `_update_settings` comes from `wp_get_referer()` (the HTTP Referer header), not user-supplied input, so there is no open redirect. The AJAX handler only writes to `wp_options` via `update_option('oe-icons', ...)` with validated values passed through `oe_validate()`.
exploitation_scenario: A subscriber-level authenticated user who has obtained a valid `oe-icons-settings-update` nonce (e.g., via CSRF or XSS on an admin page) could POST to the AJAX endpoint to modify menu icon global settings. Impact is limited to aesthetic/functional changes to nav menu icon display, not code execution.
remediation: Add `if ( ! current_user_can( 'edit_theme_options' ) ) { wp_send_json_error(); }` at the start of `_ajax_oe_menu_icons_update_settings()` before the nonce check.
false_positive_reason: Partially mitigated because the nonce is only generated for admin users on nav-menus.php. Real-world exploitability requires nonce exfiltration, making this low severity. No open redirect exists - wp_get_referer() reads the HTTP Referer header from the server, not a user parameter.

title: Library Shortcode Renders Arbitrary Post Content by ID - Not Directly Exploitable
vulnerability_class: Access Control - Not Exploitable via Direct Input
description: The `[oceanwp_library id=N]` shortcode renders content from a post of type `oceanwp_library`. The ID comes from shortcode attributes and is sanitized with `absint()`. The function validates that the post type is exactly `oceanwp_library` and the status is `publish` before rendering. It can invoke Elementor's `get_builder_content_for_display()`, SiteOrigin's `render()`, or `do_shortcode()` on the post content. This is not exploitable by external users because: (a) the id must be an integer, (b) the post must be of type `oceanwp_library` and published, and (c) shortcode attributes are set by editors/admins via post content, not by end users via URL parameters.
exploitation_scenario: N/A - exploitation would require an attacker to already have editor-level access to create an `oceanwp_library` post containing malicious content, at which point they would already have code execution capability via other means.
remediation: No action required. The implementation correctly constrains post type and status.
false_positive_reason: True false positive. The id is constrained by absint(), post type validation, and publish status check. No direct user input flows from HTTP request to this shortcode renderer.

title: Freemius Logger REQUEST_URI in wpdb->insert - Not Vulnerable
description: The `$_SERVER['REQUEST_URI']` value is passed to `$wpdb->insert()` as the `request_url` field. The `wpdb::insert()` method uses `wpdb::prepare()` internally, which escapes all values via `%s`/`%d` format specifiers before executing the SQL query. This is the documented safe way to insert user-supplied data in WordPress. The value is stored as a log entry and is only readable by admins via the Freemius debug interface. There is no SQL injection here.
exploitation_scenario: N/A - wpdb::insert() escapes all values automatically.
false_positive_reason: True false positive. wpdb::insert() internally calls prepare() and escapes all provided values regardless of their source. This is not a SQL injection vulnerability.

title: Mautic Subscription Endpoint Uses HTTP (Not HTTPS)
description: The `subscribe_to_mautic()` method hardcodes the Mautic base URL as `http://mautic.oceanwp.org` (plain HTTP). Email addresses submitted by users are transmitted over an unencrypted connection to the OceanWP Mautic server. Any network observer (ISP, shared hosting provider, network middlebox) can intercept and read the email addresses in transit.
exploitation_scenario: A network attacker performing a man-in-the-middle attack on traffic between the WordPress server and mautic.oceanwp.org can intercept POST requests containing user email addresses and mailing list subscription data. This is particularly relevant in shared hosting environments where multiple tenants share network infrastructure.
remediation: Change `$mautic_base_url = 'http://mautic.oceanwp.org'` to `$mautic_base_url = 'https://mautic.oceanwp.org'`. Verify the Mautic server has a valid TLS certificate.
false_positive_reason: Not a false positive. The HTTP URL is hardcoded and confirmed in the source.
